sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 93, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 92 actions; a strong score needs about 54 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After pressing UP from below the bar, P[3] changed from UNK back to P[5], but the cursor is still at (63, 8) - below the bar. So UP from below the bar changed P[3] but didn't move the cursor back to the P bar.
Wait, that's weird. The cursor is at (63, 8) which is below pos[1], but pressing UP changed P[3]? That doesn't make sense unless the cursor position doesn't matter for which P symbol gets changed.
Actually wait, let me re-read. Before UP: P[3] = UNK. After UP: P[3] = P[5]. So UP from below the bar changed P[3] from UNK to P[5].
But the cursor is at pos[1] (col ~8). How did it affect P[3]?
Hmm, maybe I misread. Let me check again...
Before: P[0]=P[0], P[1]=UNK, P[2]=P[5], P[3]=UNK, P[4]=P[0]
After:  P[0]=P[0], P[1]=UNK, P[2]=P[5], P[3]=P[5], P[4]=P[0]
So P[3] changed from UNK to P[5]. But the cursor is at pos[1]...
Wait, maybe the cursor position doesn't correspond to which P symbol gets changed. Maybe UP/DOWN always changes a specific position regardless of cursor position?
Or maybe I'm misreading the cursor position. Let me check more carefully.
Actually, looking at the image, the cursor (white block) appears to be below the P bar, centered under position 3 (the middle one). The column 8 might be approximate.
Let me reconsider. The cursor was at pos[3] when I pressed DOWN. After DOWN, cursor moved to below the bar. The col is ~9, which could be pos[3]'s column.
Let me check: pos[0] starts at col 14, pos[1] at 21, pos[2] at 28, pos[3] at 35, pos[4] at 42.
Center of pos[3] would be around col 35 + 3.5 = 38.5.
But the cursor is at col ~8-9. That's way off. Unless the segmentation is measuring something different.
Actually, the cursor might be a small white block at the bottom of the screen, not directly below any particular position. Let me look at the image again.
Looking at the image, I see a white block below the lower P bar, centered. It seems to be a separate UI element, not directly under any position.
Wait, there are TWO white blocks in the image: one above the lower P bar (between S and P rows) and one below the P bar. The one above is the cursor for the P bar, and the one below might be a separate indicator.
Actually, I think the white block below the P bar is the cursor. When I pressed DOWN from the P bar, the cursor moved below the P bar. And when I pressed UP from below, it changed P[3] but the cursor stayed below.
This is confusing. Let me try pressing UP again to see if it changes P[3] again (cycling through its states).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try pressing UP multiple times from below the bar to see what changes
# And track which position changes

def get_pos_ids(frame_ascii):
    """Get which upper P symbol each position shows"""
    upper_P = {
        0: 'BBBB.B..BBB...BBB..B.BBBB',
        1: 'BBBB.B..B.B..BBB..B.BBBB.',
        2: 'BBBBBB..B.B..B.BBBB.B....',
        3: '..BBB..B.BBBBBBB.B..BBB..',
        4: 'BBBBBB...BB.BBBB.B.BBBBBB',
        5: '..B..BBBBBB.B.BBBBBB..B..'
    }
    result = []
    for i in range(5):
        lines = frame_ascii.split('\n')
        col_start = 14 + i * 7
        shape = []
        for row_offset in range(1, 6):
            row = lines[51 + row_offset]
            inner = row[col_start+1:col_start+6]
            shape_row = ''.join(['B' if ch == 'B' else '.' for ch in inner])
            shape.append(shape_row)
        s = ''.join(shape)
        for j, up in upper_P.items():
            if s == up:
                result.append(j)
                break
        else:
            result.append(-1)  # UNKNOWN
    return result

print(f"Before: {get_pos_ids(current_frame.ascii)}")

# Press UP 6 times and track changes
for k in range(6):
    action(['UP'])
    ids = get_pos_ids(current_frame.ascii)
    print(f"After UP {k+1}: {ids}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
